1: 2 Samuel 22 is David's victory hymn — the song of the anointed king (māšîaḥ, v51) who was delivered from all his enemies. It stands at the end of the Samuel narrative as the interpretive key to the entire David story. In its deepest register, it is the song Christ himself will sing — cited in Hebrews 2:12 ('I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise') as the risen Son's praise among his people. The ultimate singer of this psalm is not David but the Davidic Messiah who was delivered from death itself.
2: 'My rock, my fortress, my deliverer.' Christ as the Rock is the NT's direct application of this cluster: 1 Corinthians 10:4 ('the Rock was Christ'); Matthew 7:24-25 (the wise man who builds on the rock); 1 Peter 2:6-8 (Christ as the cornerstone, both precious foundation and stumbling stone). The geological metaphors David uses — rock, fortress, stronghold — become in the NT the vocabulary for Christ as the ultimate foundation of the new covenant community. What YHWH was to David (the rock who did not move), Christ is to his people.
3: 'My God, my rock, in whom I take refuge, my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my stronghold and my refuge, my savior.' The horn of salvation (qeren yišʿî) is the precise phrase Zechariah uses in his Benedictus: 'He has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David' (Luke 1:69). Zechariah's Spirit-inspired proclamation at John the Baptist's birth identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of what David was singing about — the 'horn of salvation' David celebrated is the Davidic Messiah, Jesus of Nazareth.
4: 'I call upon YHWH, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.' Christ is the one who called upon YHWH in the days of his flesh — 'he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence' (Hebrews 5:7). The pattern of David's cry-and-rescue is fulfilled in Gethsemane and Easter morning: the anointed king cried to YHWH, was heard, and was delivered — not from the cross, but through it and out the other side.
5: 'For the waves of death encompassed me, the torrents of destruction assailed me.' The cords/waves of death surrounding the psalmist find their ultimate fulfillment in the one who descended into death itself. The NT confesses that Christ experienced the full force of ḥeḇlê māweṯ — not merely near-death but actual death. 'He tasted death for everyone' (Hebrews 2:9); he was 'delivered up to death' (Romans 4:25). What David describes as mortal danger surrounding him, Christ entered completely — so that he could emerge from the other side as the firstborn from the dead.
6: 'The cords of Sheol entangled me; the snares of death confronted me.' The language of Sheol's cords and death's snares is the language Christ exhausted and overcame. Acts 2:24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' The 'pangs/cords' (ṓdines in LXX = ḥeḇlîm) of death that Peter describes being loosed at the resurrection are the same cords of Sheol that 2 Samuel 22:6 describes surrounding David. Christ is the one who entered Sheol's grip and burst it open.
7: 'In my distress I called upon YHWH; to my God I called. From his temple he heard my voice.' Christ's prayer was heard — 'he was heard because of his reverence' (Hebrews 5:7). The cross and empty tomb are the proof that YHWH heard the cry of his anointed Son. John 11:42: 'I knew that you always hear me.' The Gethsemane prayer ('Father, if possible, let this cup pass') was answered not by removal of the cup but by the resurrection that followed the drinking of it. YHWH's hearing from his heavenly temple means not prevention of suffering but vindication through it.
8: The cosmic theophany of vv8-16 — earth shaking, smoke, fire, darkened skies, YHWH the divine warrior responding to his anointed's cry — is the pattern that Revelation uses for Christ's return as the divine warrior (Revelation 19:11-16). The theophany-response to the anointed king's cry is the template for the Parousia: just as YHWH descended in cosmic power to rescue David, Christ will come in power and great glory to vindicate his people. The language of Matthew 24:29-30 (sun darkened, powers shaken, the Son of Man coming on clouds) is the fulfillment-theophany of 2 Samuel 22:8-16.
9: 'Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth.' The divine warrior's fire that protects his anointed is the same fire that will consume his enemies at the end. 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8: 'the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God.' The fire of YHWH's intervention for David is the prototype of the final fire of Christ's judgment — protective for the righteous, consuming for the ungodly. The same reality experienced differently depending on one's covenant status.
10: 'He bowed the heavens and came down.' The descent theophany points to the incarnation as the supreme divine descent. The language of 2 Samuel 22:10 — YHWH bowing the heavens to come to his servant's rescue — is transformed by the incarnation: the ultimate 'coming down' is not a theophanic storm but the Word becoming flesh (John 1:14). Philippians 2:7-8: he 'emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men.' The cosmic descent of YHWH to rescue David is the shadow of the incarnational descent of the Son to rescue humanity.
11: 'He rode on a cherub and flew; he was seen on the wings of the wind.' The divine warrior riding the cherubim-throne is the imagery behind the ascension and Parousia. Acts 1:9: 'as they were looking on, he was lifted up, and a cloud took him out of their sight.' Revelation 14:14: 'one like a son of man, sitting on the cloud.' The rider-on-clouds motif from the ANE, which 2 Sam 22 applies to YHWH coming to rescue David, is applied in Revelation to Christ's universal lordship. The cloud-rider who responds to David's cry is identified in the NT as the Son of Man.
12: 'He made darkness around him his canopy, thick clouds, a gathering of water.' The darkness around YHWH in theophany reaches its fulfillment in the three hours of darkness at the cross (Matthew 27:45; Luke 23:44-45). The darkness that conceals divine glory in the OT theophanies becomes the darkness of divine judgment and concealment at Calvary — the moment when the Father's face was hidden from the Son ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me,' Psalms 22:1 / Matthew 27:46). The darkened theophany of rescue becomes the darkened theophany of the cross-judgment.
13: 'Out of the brightness before him hailstones and coals of fire broke through the clouds.' The fire from YHWH that protects David from his enemies is the same fire that reveals truth. Christ himself is the one who 'baptizes with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Matthew 3:11; Luke 3:16) — the Pentecost fire (Acts 2:3, tongues of fire distributed) is the arrival of the divine warrior's fire-presence, now poured out not for military victory over nations but for the empowerment of the covenant community for witness.
14: 'YHWH thundered from heaven, and the Most High uttered his voice.' The divine voice from heaven — the thunder that signals YHWH's response to his anointed — is heard three times in the Gospel accounts as the Father's direct speech: at Jesus's baptism ('You are my beloved Son,' Mark 1:11), at the transfiguration ('This is my beloved Son; listen to him,' Mark 9:7), and in John 12:28 ('I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again'), where bystanders mistake it for thunder. The divine voice that answers David's cry culminates in the Father's voice answering and identifying his Son.
15: 'And he sent out arrows and scattered them; lightning, and routed them.' Christ's conquest of his enemies is not military but consists in driving out the ruler of this world (John 12:31), binding the strong man (Matthew 12:29), and disarming principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15). The divine warrior's lightning-arrows that scatter David's enemies are the type of Christ's authoritative word — 'the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God' (Ephesians 6:17; Hebrews 4:12: 'the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword').
16: 'Then the channels of the sea were seen, and the foundations of the world were laid bare, at the rebuke of YHWH.' The cosmic exposure of sea-floor and earth-foundations at YHWH's rebuke echoes the Exodus sea-crossing and points to Christ's resurrection as the definitive cosmic event. Revelation 20:11: 'from his presence earth and sky fled away, and no place was found for them.' The final judgment dissolves the present order — sea giving up its dead, death and Hades giving up theirs (Revelation 20:13) — which is the eschatological fulfillment of the cosmic disruption 2 Samuel 22:16 describes at the level of an individual rescue.
17: 'He sent from on high, he took me; he drew me out of many waters.' Christ is the one who descended to draw his people out of the many waters of death and chaos. The 'drawn-out-one' motif (the name Moses = Mōšeh, drawn from the water) finds its ultimate fulfillment in the resurrection: Christ is pulled out of the waters of death, and in his resurrection he draws his people with him — 'if we have died with him, we will also live with him' (2 Timothy 2:11; cf. Romans 6:3-5).
18: 'He rescued me from my strong enemy, from those who hated me, for they were too mighty for me.' This is the Christological key to substitution: the enemy was too strong for David; YHWH rescued him. For humanity, the enemy — sin, death, the law's condemnation — was too strong for any human to overcome. Christ enters the arena where the enemy has overwhelmed humanity and conquers from within. 1 Corinthians 15:26: 'the last enemy to be destroyed is death.' Christ defeats the enemy that was too mighty for us, doing what we could not do for ourselves.
19: 'They confronted me in the day of my calamity, but YHWH was my support.' Christ's 'day of calamity' was the cross — the day when all the forces of opposition confronted him simultaneously (betrayal, abandonment, false trial, torture, death). Yet 'YHWH was my support' — Acts 2:24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' The Father's support of the Son at the moment of maximum calamity is the resurrection. Every experience of divine support in the David narrative is a rehearsal for the ultimate support: Easter morning.
20: 'He brought me out into a broad place; he rescued me, because he delighted in me.' The Father's delight in the Son is the explicit language of the baptism and transfiguration: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17; 2 Samuel 17:5). The rescue of the anointed king 'because he delighted in me' is the grace-ground of the resurrection: the Father raised the Son not because of human merit but because of the eternal love-relationship between Father and Son. The Son's rescue is the overflow of the Father's delight — and that delight extends to all who are in the Son (Ephesians 1:6: 'accepted in the beloved').
21: 'YHWH dealt with me according to my righteousness.' Where David's righteousness claims (vv21-25) are partial and provisional — he is covenant-faithful relative to his enemies but not sinlessly perfect — Christ's righteousness is absolute and complete. He is 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners' (Hebrews 7:26). The righteousness that David could only approximate, Christ embodies fully. And the exchange: 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). David's righteousness claim becomes Christ's actual righteousness imputed to his people.
22: 'For I have kept the ways of YHWH and have not wickedly departed from my God.' Christ is the one who kept all YHWH's ways without exception — 'I always do the things that are pleasing to him' (John 8:29); 'I have kept my Father's commandments and abide in his love' (John 15:10). The NT's claim that Jesus fulfilled 'all righteousness' (Matthew 3:15) is the fulfillment of David's psalm: where David kept YHWH's ways imperfectly and provisionally, Christ kept them perfectly and permanently — establishing a righteousness that his people receive by faith rather than generate by performance.
23: 'For all his rules were before me, and his statutes I did not put away from me.' The ideal Deuteronomic king who meditates on the Torah day and night (Deuteronomy 17:19; Psalms 1:2) finds its fulfillment in Christ, who is himself the incarnate Word. John 1:1: 'In the beginning was the Word.' He did not merely observe Torah externally; he is the Torah's living embodiment. Matthew 5:17: 'I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them.' Every statute and rule is not only before Christ's eyes but is expressed in his person and life.
24: 'I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt.' Tāmîm (blameless/perfect) is the character standard that only Christ fully meets. Hebrews 4:15: 'one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.' 1 Peter 1:19: 'a lamb without blemish or spot.' Where David's blamelessness is covenant-faithfulness with ongoing moral failure requiring confession and restoration, Christ's blamelessness is absolute — the spotless sacrifice whose unblemished character makes him the perfect offering for the sins of those who are not blameless.
25: 'Therefore YHWH has recompensed me according to my righteousness.' The covenant principle — faithfulness receives vindication — is exhaustively fulfilled in the resurrection. The resurrection is the Father's public declaration that Christ's righteousness was accepted, his sacrifice was sufficient, his death was expiatory. Acts 17:31: God 'has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead.' The resurrection is the divine recompense for the righteous life and atoning death of the anointed Son — the covenant principle of vv21-25 working at the level of eschatological vindication.
26: 'With the merciful you show yourself merciful; with the blameless man you show yourself blameless.' The divine-mirror principle finds its ultimate expression in Christ's mediation. The mercy Christ shows — 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34) — is the mirror of the Father's mercy. Christ mediates the mercy of God to those who are not themselves merciful or blameless, enabling them to receive the mercy they would otherwise be excluded from. He stands in the gap as the blameless one on behalf of those who are not blameless.
27: 'With the pure you show yourself pure, and with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous.' Christ's purity is the standard against which all human crookedness is revealed. John 3:19-20: 'the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil.' The coming of the Pure One into a crooked world both reveals the crookedness and provides the remedy — the same light that exposes is the light that heals. His purity is not a rebuke to be endured but a grace to be received.
28: 'You save a humble people, but your eyes are on the haughty to bring them down.' Christ is the embodiment of the humble people who are saved — 'I am gentle and lowly in heart' (Matthew 11:29); 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death' (Philippians 2:8). And Christ is the agent of the bringing-down of the haughty: his ministry consistently overturns human hierarchies of honor (Luke 14:11; 2 Samuel 18:14). The divine principle of v28 is not an abstract law but a Person — the humble one who was exalted (Philippians 2:9) and who saves all who come to him in humility.
29: 'For you are my lamp, O YHWH, and my God lightens my darkness.' Christ is 'the light of the world' (John 8:12; 2 Samuel 9:5) — the ultimate fulfillment of the lamp imagery. YHWH who was David's lamp is now incarnate in the one who says 'I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' The lamp metaphor for divine guidance (Psalms 119:105: 'your word is a lamp to my feet') culminates in the person of Christ, in whom the divine Word and divine Light are identical (John 1:1-9).
30: 'For by you I can run against a troop, and by my God I can leap over a wall.' Christ's authority enables his followers to do what they cannot do by natural capacity — 'I can do all things through him who strengthens me' (Philippians 4:13). The divine-empowerment principle (David runs against troops only through YHWH) is fulfilled in the Spirit's empowerment of the church: Acts 1:8, 'you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you.' The impossible mission — proclaiming the gospel to all nations — is accomplished through the same divine-empowerment principle David sings about here.
31: 'This God — his way is perfect; the word of YHWH proves true; he is a shield for all those who take refuge in him.' Christ is 'the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8) — the perfect way (darKô tāmîm) made personal. John 14:6: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life.' The 'way of YHWH' that is perfect is not merely a path but the Person of the Son. The word that proves true (ṣĕrûpâ, refined/tested) is identified in John 1:1 as the eternal Word who became flesh — the tested-and-proven Word now accessible in human form.
32: 'For who is God, but YHWH? And who is a rock, except our God?' The monotheistic claim of the psalm is fulfilled in the NT's confession of Christ's divine identity. John 10:30: 'I and the Father are one.' The rock — YHWH's exclusive claim in 2 Sam 22 — is applied to Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4 ('the Rock was Christ') and 1 Peter 2:6-8 (Christ as the cornerstone). The theological move is stunning: the one who in 2 Sam 22 is called YHWH-the-only-rock is the same one Paul and Peter identify as Christ. The psalm's monotheism is fulfilled in Trinitarian Christology.
33: 'This God is my strong refuge and has made my way blameless.' Christ is the refuge of his people — Hebrews 6:18: 'we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us.' He is also the one who makes his people's way blameless — not by their own achievement but by imputed righteousness: 'you are in Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption' (1 Corinthians 1:30). The blameless way God makes for his people runs through the blameless Person of his Son.
34: 'He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.' The sure-footed security on dangerous terrain is the spiritual reality Christ gives his people: 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The security of covenant relationship with the divine shepherd — standing firm on heights that would otherwise be fatal — is the believer's position in Christ. Jude 24: 'to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory.'
35: 'He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.' Christ as the one who equips for spiritual warfare: 'Put on the whole armor of God' (Ephesians 6:11-17). The armor Paul lists — truth, righteousness, gospel-readiness, faith, salvation, the word of God — is the spiritual equipment corresponding to the physical equipping David describes. The Warrior-King who trained David's hands for physical warfare is the risen Christ who equips his people for the spiritual warfare of the age between his resurrection and return.
36: 'You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your gentleness made me great.' The 'shield of salvation' (Ephesians 6:16: 'the shield of faith') and the divine gentleness/meekness (ʿanwâ) that makes great are both Christological. Christ is the Gentle One (Matthew 11:29: 'I am gentle and lowly in heart') whose gentleness is itself the vehicle of his power. The counter-intuitive claim — that divine meekness makes great — is the theology of the cross: the weakness of God is stronger than human strength (1 Corinthians 1:25), and the meek inherit the earth (Matthew 5:5).
37: 'You gave a wide place for my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.' Christ gives his people secure footing through the Spirit's internal work. Romans 8:38-39: 'neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.' The spacious-place security that David experiences as YHWH's covenant faithfulness to him is, for believers, the unassailable security of being united to the one who died, rose, and intercedes for them.
38: 'I pursued my enemies and destroyed them, and did not turn back until they were consumed.' Christ's pursuit and destruction of his enemies is described in NT eschatological terms: 1 Corinthians 15:25-26: 'he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death.' The relentless pursuit that David describes as military victory is the pattern of Christ's cosmic rule — he does not rest until every enemy is subdued. The final enemy (death) is destroyed at the resurrection of the dead, completing the victory that began on Easter morning.
39: 'I consumed them; I thrust them through, so that they did not rise; they fell under my feet.' The enemies-under-feet imagery is the NT's primary lens for understanding Christ's exaltation. Psalms 110:1 ('sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool') is the most-cited OT verse in the NT. Ephesians 1:22: 'he put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church.' The fallen-enemies-under-feet of David's victory hymn is the pattern of Christ's post-resurrection authority — all things subject to the exalted Son, for the benefit of his body.
40: 'For you equipped me with strength for the battle; you made those who rise against me sink under me.' The Christ who was himself equipped by the Father — 'The Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak' (John 12:49); 'the Father is greater than I' (John 14:28) — is the Son who acts in and through the Father's empowering. The incarnational kenosis (Philippians 2:7: he 'emptied himself') means that the Son accomplished his mission in human dependence on the Father's equipping — the same divine-empowerment structure David describes in v40.
41: 'You made my enemies turn their backs to me, those who hated me, and I destroyed them.' The turning of enemies before the anointed king is the pattern fulfilled in Christ's resurrection triumph. Colossians 2:15: 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.' The cross appeared to be the ultimate defeat — the enemies prevailing — but the resurrection revealed it as the ultimate victory: the powers that thought they had won turned their backs and were publicly exposed as defeated. The great reversal of Easter is the fulfillment of v41.
42: 'They looked, but there was none to save; they cried to YHWH, but he did not answer them.' The unanswered cry of those who are outside covenant relationship points to the seriousness of the final judgment. Matthew 7:22-23: 'Many will say to me, Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name … then will I declare to them, I never knew you.' The same God who answers David's cry and Christ's prayer does not answer those who are not in covenant relationship with him — not arbitrary exclusion but the structural consequence of having rejected the covenant. The unanswered cry of v42 is the shadow of the 'outer darkness' in Jesus's parables.
43: 'I beat them fine as the dust of the earth; I crushed them and stamped them down like the mire of the streets.' The complete subjugation of enemies reaches its ultimate form in the final judgment, where all anti-covenant powers are destroyed. Revelation 20:14: 'death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire.' The crushing and stamping that David sings as military victory is the type of Christ's ultimate conquest — not a military campaign but the final dissolution of everything that opposes the reign of God. The dust imagery points to Genesis 3:14 (the serpent's head crushed).
44: 'You delivered me from strife with my people; you kept me as the head of the nations; people whom I had not known served me.' Christ is 'the head of all rule and authority' (Colossians 2:10); 'the ruler of kings on earth' (Revelation 1:5). The nations served David; the nations belong to Christ — 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18). The universal lordship that David sings about as a covenant expansion (from one people to all nations) is fulfilled in Christ's cosmic headship: the one who was rejected by his own people (John 1:11) is installed as Lord of all.
45: 'Foreigners came cringing to me; as soon as they heard of me, they obeyed me.' The obedience of the nations to the Davidic king at the report of his deeds is the missional template: 'the obedience of faith among all the nations' (Romans 1:5) that Paul describes as his apostolic commission. The Gentile mission is the fulfillment of v45 — foreigners who hear of Christ's deeds (his death and resurrection) come in faith-submission. The cringing before David becomes the bowing of every knee (Philippians 2:10) before the name of Jesus, the Davidic Messiah.
46: 'Foreigners lost heart and came trembling out of their fortresses.' The nations' submission to the Davidic king reaches its eschatological form in Revelation 21:24-26: 'the nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it.' The nations who brought tribute to David — sometimes by compulsion — will bring their glory to the New Jerusalem voluntarily. The trembling-submission of v46 is transformed: not conquest-surrender but the voluntary honoring of the Lamb by the redeemed from every tribe and tongue and people and nation (Revelation 5:9).
47: 'YHWH lives!' — the affirmation of the living God is fulfilled in the resurrection confession. Romans 14:9: 'Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living.' The cry ḥay YHWH is the OT's equivalent of the NT's resurrection confession. The God who answered David's cry by demonstrating that he is living — not the silent dead idols of the nations — demonstrates his living nature supremely in raising Jesus from the dead. 'He is not the God of the dead, but of the living' (Matthew 22:32) applies to the resurrection of Christ what 2 Samuel 22:47 affirms about YHWH.
48: 'The God who gave me vengeance and brought down peoples under me.' The vengeance-and-vindication theme finds its ultimate expression in Christ's resurrection and final judgment. Romans 12:19 (Deuteronomy 32:35): 'vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.' Christ does not take personal revenge; he entrusts judgment to the Father. But the Father's vindication of the crucified Son is itself the ultimate act of covenant justice: the one who was unjustly condemned is declared righteous by the resurrection (1 Timothy 3:16: 'vindicated by the Spirit').
49: 'Who rescued me from my enemies; yes, you exalted me above those who rose against me; you delivered me from the man of violence.' The triple rescue statement — from enemies, from those who rise against, from the violent — covers the full spectrum of Christ's opponents: the religious establishment, the Roman imperial power, and the devil (the ultimate 'man of violence'). All three conspired at the cross; all three were overcome by the resurrection. 1 John 3:8: 'the reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil.' The rescue of v49 is the destruction of all Christ's enemies, which is simultaneously the rescue of his people.
50: 'For this I will praise you, YHWH, among the nations, and sing to your name.' Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 15:9 as one of four OT texts proving that the Gentile mission was always YHWH's plan. The full chain: the Davidic king sings praise among the nations (2 Samuel 22:50); the Gentiles rejoice with God's people (Deuteronomy 32:43); all peoples praise YHWH (Psalms 117:1); the root of Jesse arises to rule the nations (Isaiah 11:10). Christ is the Davidic king of v50 who leads the praise of the nations — Hebrews 2:12: 'I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise.'
51: 'Great salvation he brings to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to David and his offspring forever.' The closing verse is the theological summit of 2 Samuel 22 and the entire David narrative. Māšîaḥô — his anointed one, his Messiah. The ḥesed to the Davidic offspring ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) is the Davidic covenant in its most concentrated form. Galatians 3:16 applies the same seed-logic that 2 Sam 7 establishes: 'to your offspring,' which is Christ. The eternal covenant-loyalty YHWH pledges to David's seed is fulfilled in the resurrection-life of the one seed who is both David's son and David's Lord (Mark 12:35-37). The last word of the David narrative is the first word of Christology.
